summary: Deep regression audit of the freshest 2026-06-04 diffs (face lock-restructure + perf changes)
logs: Recent-diff audit done: 4 confirmed, 0 uncertain (of 4 raw)

confirmed.title: New `face_clustering_busy` error kind wrongly releases the app's auto-clustering single-flight gate (substring "cluster" collision)
confirmed.description: Commit d7b0159f added a NEW engine error kind `face_clustering_busy` (main.rs:448, emitted when the new single-flight guard bounces a concurrent runFaceClustering). The app's ErrorEvent handler releases the auto-clustering re-entrancy gate with `if (e.Error.Kind is { } ck && ck.Contains("cluster", StringComparison.OrdinalIgnoreCase)) Interlocked.Exchange(ref _faceClusterAutoInFlight, 0);`. That `Contains("cluster")` predicate predates this commit (added in 550cd194, when the ONLY clustering error kind was `face_clustering_failed`). The new `face_clustering_busy` string ALSO contains "cluster", so a bounced-busy event now matches and clears `_faceClusterAutoInFlight`. But `face_clustering_busy` means a clustering run is STILL ACTIVE on the engine — the opposite of a failure — so the gate must NOT be released. Concrete sequence: (1) ScanComplete -> AutoTriggerFaceClusteringAsync sets gate=1, sends RunFaceClustering; engine sets face_cluster_active=true, Run A starts (multi-second). (2) User clicks Re-cluster in the People tab -> RunFaceClusteringAsync sends a second RunFaceClustering directly (this path does NOT go through AutoTrigger, so the app gate is never consulted). (3) Engine single-flight bounces it -> emits face_clustering_busy. (4) ErrorEvent handler matches Contains("cluster") and sets gate=0 while Run A is still running. (5) A second ScanComplete (rescan) now passes AutoTrigger's CompareExchange(1,0) because the gate was wrongly cleared, sends yet another RunFaceClustering, which the engine ALSO bounces with face_clustering_busy if Run A is still active -> the rescan's newly-detected faces never get auto-clustered until a later manual trigger. The engine-side guard prevents an actual data race, but the app's single-flight semantics are broken and the post-rescan auto-cluster is silently dropped.
confirmed.impact: Auto-clustering after a rescan can be silently skipped when a manual Re-cluster (or any second RunFaceClustering) is bounced as busy while a prior pass is still running. New faces from the rescan are then not surfaced in the People tab until the user manually re-clusters again. The app-side single-flight gate no longer reliably reflects whether a clustering pass is in flight. No crash, no data corruption (engine guard backstops the race), but a real lost-update of the People view.
confirmed.fix: Release the auto gate only on a genuine clustering FAILURE, not on the busy bounce. Replace the broad substring test with the exact failure kind: `if (e.Error.Kind == "face_clustering_failed") { Interlocked.Exchange(ref _faceClusterAutoInFlight, 0); }`. (face_clustering_busy is already classified as a non-fatal warning in IsNonFatalWarningKind and must leave the gate untouched, since the in-flight Run A will emit its own FaceClusteringComplete/face_clustering_failed that legitimately releases the gate.)

verdict.reasoning:
VERIFIED as a real, commit-d7b0159f-introduced regression. All load-bearing claims check out against the current code and the git history.

1. NEW error kind is real and contains "cluster": d7b0159f added `emit_face_clustering_busy` emitting kind="face_clustering_busy" (platforms/windows/src/engine/src/main.rs:446-454), sent on the single-flight bounce (main.rs:796) when a second RunFaceClustering arrives while one is active (CompareExchange at 787-794). The string "face_clustering_busy" contains the substring "cluster".

2. The gate-release predicate is pre-existing and now over-broad: `if (e.Error.Kind is { } ck && ck.Contains("cluster", StringComparison.OrdinalIgnoreCase)) Interlocked.Exchange(ref _faceClusterAutoInFlight, 0);` at platforms/windows/src/FileID.App/ViewModels/EngineClient.cs:1077-1080. I confirmed via `git show 550cd194:...EngineClient.cs` that this exact `Contains("cluster")` test existed BEFORE d7b0159f (line 1045 there), when the ONLY clustering error kind was `face_clustering_failed` (commands/face_clustering.rs:388,403). So before d7b0159f the predicate could only match a genuine FAILURE — releasing the gate was correct. d7b0159f introduced a second "cluster"-bearing kind without narrowing the predicate. Classic regression shape: a new string the old predicate now wrongly matches.

3. The release runs unconditionally w.r.t. the warning/error split: line 1077 sits AFTER the `if (IsNonFatalWarningKind) { LastWarning=... } else { LastError=... }` block, so a `face_clustering_busy` warning (classified non-fatal in EngineClient.Commands.cs:161) still falls through to the Contains test and clears the gate.

4. Reachability of a SECOND RunFaceClustering that bypasses the app gate is confirmed: PeopleView.OnRefreshClicked (Views/People/PeopleView.xaml.cs:324) calls `RunFaceClusteringAsync()` directly — it does NOT go through AutoTriggerFaceClusteringAsync, so the `_faceClusterAutoInFlight` CompareExchange gate (EngineClient.cs:1224) is never consulted. A manual Re-cluster during an active auto-pass therefore reaches the engine, is bounced busy, and wrongly clears the gate.

5. The clear is both wrong AND unnecessary: the in-flight Run A always emits its own terminal event — FaceClusteringComplete (face_clustering.rs:380) or face_clustering_failed (388/403, incl. the panic/JoinError path) — each of which legitimately releases the gate (EngineClient.cs:1088 / 1077). So nothing requires the busy bounce to release it.

Impact (honestly bounded): the engine-side guard backstops the actual data race (a bounced request returns before constructing FaceClusterActiveGuard, so engine state stays correct), so there is NO crash and NO DB corruption. The damage is purely to the app's single-flight semantics: the gate stops reflecting whether a pass is in flight. Concretely, after a busy bounce clears the gate mid-Run-A, a rescan's ScanComplete AutoTrigger now passes CompareExchange(1,0) and fires a redundant RunFaceClustering that the engine bounces busy too. Because face_clustering.rs phase-1 reads under the lock and any face inserted after that read lands person_id=NULL "picked up next run" (documented at face_clustering.rs:26-28), the rescan's freshly-detected NULL-person faces are then not auto-assigned until a later successful clustering run — a real (if recoverable) lost-update of the People view. The trigger window is narrow (requires a manual Re-cluster or other second RunFaceClustering DURING an active auto-pass), and NULL faces are eventually absorbed by any subsequent successful run, which is why this is medium, not high.

The proposed fix is correct and complete: narrowing to `e.Error.Kind == "face_clustering_failed"` still catches both genuine-failure emitters (face_clustering.rs:388 and the panic-recovery 402-408) while leaving the gate untouched on the busy bounce, letting Run A's own terminal event release it.
verdict.fix_assessment: The proposed fix is correct and minimal. Replacing the broad substring test with an exact match on the genuine failure kind restores the pre-d7b0159f behavior (only a real clustering failure releases the auto gate) while leaving the new non-fatal busy bounce untouched. It does not regress the panic-recovery path: the spawn-failure/JoinError branch (face_clustering.rs:402-408) also emits exactly "face_clustering_failed", so the exact match still catches it. No other error kind needs to release this gate.

confirmed.title: BGE text encoder pinned to CPU EP but still tuned for GPU (1 intra-op thread) on every GPU box — single-threaded CPU inference regression
confirmed.description: Commit c07f93e8 changed BGE's EP chain from `priority_chain(probe.vendor)` (which selects the box's GPU EP — CUDA/DirectML/OpenVINO/QNN — on a GPU box) to a hardcoded `vec![ExecutionProvider::Cpu]` so BGE no longer consumes VRAM. The problem is the thread-count tuning: `configure_session_builder(builder)` is called at bge_text.rs:56, and that function (runtime.rs:~370-395, unchanged by this commit) does NOT key off the chain BGE passes. It independently recomputes the EP as `priority_chain(RuntimeProbe::shared().vendor).into_iter().next()` — i.e. the BOX's preferred EP — and sets `intra_threads = 1` whenever that EP is not `Cpu` (the GPU/NPU branch: 'the accelerator does the parallelism'). On any GPU box (NVIDIA/AMD/Intel/Snapdragon, including the 6 GB RTX 2060 reference box) `priority_chain().next()` is the GPU EP, so `intra = 1`. Meanwhile BGE's `[Cpu]` chain yields an empty providers vec (the Cpu arm in execution_providers_for_chain is a no-op, runtime.rs:304), so `with_execution_providers` is skipped and the session falls back to ORT's implicit CPU EP. Net: BGE now runs on the CPU but throttled to a single intra-op thread. Before the commit, BGE ran on the GPU (where 1 thread is correct). The commit's own comment ('CPU is ORT's implicit fallback ... only the EP differs') and `configure_session_builder`'s doc ('on the CPU EP, use all performance cores so CPU-only boxes get multi-threaded inference; the wrappers previously pinned 1, leaving CPU users single-threaded and badly underutilized') show the intent was multi-threaded CPU — but BGE hits the worst of both: CPU execution with the GPU's 1-thread setting.
confirmed.impact: BGE embeds document/PDF text per-file during a scan (called from pipeline/tagging.rs:1251-1253 `bge.embed(&text)`). On a doc-heavy library this path is now single-threaded CPU on every GPU box instead of GPU-accelerated, materially slowing the semantic-doc-index portion of a scan. This is also NOT tier-gated, so it changes the 6 GB RTX 2060 reference box (the commit claims hardware-sensitive knobs leave that box 'byte-identical' — BGE pinning is an ungated exception that moves it from GPU to single-threaded CPU). No correctness change (same weights, same output, CPU vs GPU numerically equivalent), purely throughput.
confirmed.fix: Make the thread-count follow the session's actual EP, not the box vendor. Cleanest: have `configure_session_builder` take the intended EP (or the chain) as a parameter and pass `ExecutionProvider::Cpu` from BgeText::load so it selects the all-p-cores branch; alternatively give BGE a dedicated builder step that overrides intra_threads to `cpu_topology().p_cores.max(1)` after `configure_session_builder`. Either way, when BGE is CPU-pinned it must get the multi-threaded CPU intra-op count, matching the documented 'CPU-only boxes get multi-threaded inference' intent.

verdict.reasoning:
Verified the mechanism end-to-end against the diff and current code.

BEFORE c07f93e8 (bge_text.rs): `let chain = priority_chain(probe.vendor); ... execution_providers_for_chain(&chain, probe.adapter_index)`. On any GPU box the chain leads with the GPU EP (priority_chain, runtime.rs:148-167), which emits a real ExecutionProviderDispatch, so BGE bound to and ran on the GPU. `configure_session_builder` set intra_threads=1, which is correct for a GPU EP.

AFTER c07f93e8 (bge_text.rs:53,57): `let chain = vec![ExecutionProvider::Cpu]; ... execution_providers_for_chain(&chain, None)`. The Cpu arm of execution_providers_for_chain is a no-op (runtime.rs:304), so `providers` is empty; bge_text.rs:58 then skips `with_execution_providers`, and ORT falls back to its implicit CPU EP. BGE now executes on CPU.

The bug: `configure_session_builder` (runtime.rs:370-396, UNCHANGED by this commit) does NOT key intra_threads off the chain BGE passes. It independently recomputes the EP as `priority_chain(RuntimeProbe::shared().vendor).into_iter().next()` (runtime.rs:381-384) — the BOX vendor's preferred EP — and sets `intra = 1` whenever that EP != Cpu (runtime.rs:390-394). On any GPU box (NVIDIA/AMD/Intel/Snapdragon, including the RTX 2060 reference box) next() is the GPU EP, so intra=1. Net: BGE now runs on CPU but throttled to a single intra-op thread.

This is reachable: tagging.rs:1250-1253 calls `bge.embed(&text)` per Doc/PDF file during every scan. The path now runs single-threaded CPU on every GPU box.

The function's own doc (runtime.rs:365-369) states the design intent — "on the CPU EP, use all performance cores so CPU-only boxes get multi-threaded inference ... the wrappers previously pinned 1, leaving CPU users single-threaded and badly underutilized" — yet BGE, the one model now intentionally CPU-pinned, never reaches the multi-threaded CPU branch because the tuner reads box vendor instead of the session's actual chain. The commit therefore silently undershoots its own intended design (CPU-multi-thread) and is also a strict regression vs the prior GPU execution.

Refutation attempts that FAILED to refute: (1) Empty-providers → implicit CPU EP is confirmed by both the code comment (runtime.rs:247-250) and the skipped with_execution_providers branch. (2) No correctness change — same weights, mean-pool + L2-normalize are pure host math (bge_text.rs:125-146), CPU/GPU numerically equivalent. The finding correctly scopes this as throughput-only. (3) The before-state really was GPU on a GPU box, so the regression is genuine, not a no-op.

The finding's technical claims (file/line, EP resolution, thread-count logic, call site) are all accurate.
verdict.fix_assessment: The finding's mechanism and root cause are correct, and its fix direction is sound: the thread count must follow the session's ACTUAL EP, not the box vendor. One caveat on the proposed "make configure_session_builder take the intended EP" variant: that function is shared by 8 wrappers (mobileclip, ram_plus, clip_text, scrfd, sface, yunet, bge_text), all of which currently rely on the vendor-keyed behavior, so parameterizing it requires touching every call site and passing the correct per-model EP. The lower-risk fix is a BGE-local override of intra_threads after configure_session_builder, leaving the other wrappers untouched. Severity: the prior auditor said medium; honest real-world impact is LOW — it is confined to the BGE doc/PDF text-embedding path (not the image hot path that dominates scans), there is zero correctness impact, and the GPU→CPU move itself was a deliberate VRAM-saving design choice. The only introduced harm is the single-threaded throttle of that CPU path, which is material only on doc-heavy corpora. Medium is defensible for a doc-heavy library; low is the honest expected-case severity.

confirmed.title: New face_clustering_busy warning kind spuriously releases the PAR-111 auto-clustering re-entrancy gate
confirmed.description:
Commit d7b0159f added the new engine error kind "face_clustering_busy" (engine emits it when a concurrent runFaceClustering bounces off the new single-flight guard face_cluster_active, see main.rs:446-448 / 787-798) and whitelisted it in IsNonFatalWarningKind (EngineClient.Commands.cs:161). But the ErrorEvent handler at EngineClient.cs:1077 releases the auto-clustering re-entrancy gate on ANY error kind whose string contains "cluster": `if (e.Error.Kind is { } ck && ck.Contains("cluster", StringComparison.OrdinalIgnoreCase)) Interlocked.Exchange(ref _faceClusterAutoInFlight, 0);`. "face_clustering_busy".Contains("cluster") is true. That Contains-check predates this commit (added 550cd194) and was written to catch genuine clustering-FAILURE kinds, where releasing the gate is correct. d7b0159f introduced the first BENIGN "*cluster*" kind, so the substring test now mis-fires on it.

Concrete race: (1) an auto pass fires on ScanComplete -> AutoTriggerFaceClusteringAsync sets _faceClusterAutoInFlight=1 and sends runFaceClustering; the engine sets face_cluster_active=true and spawns the long clustering task. (2) While it runs, the user clicks Re-cluster in People (PeopleView.xaml.cs:324 calls RunFaceClusteringAsync() directly, which does NOT touch the gate). The engine's compare_exchange fails and emits face_clustering_busy. (3) The app's ErrorEvent handler sets LastWarning (correct) AND, because the kind contains "cluster", Interlocked.Exchange(ref _faceClusterAutoInFlight, 0) -- it clears the auto gate WHILE the real auto pass is still running on the engine. (4) A subsequent ScanComplete (rescan) now sees the gate at 0 and fires a second runFaceClustering, defeating PAR-111's dedup precisely in the window it exists to protect (two passes racing the same persons / face_prints.person_id writes). FaceClusteringCompleteEvent from the original auto pass will also redundantly Exchange the gate to 0 later, so the flag is left in an inconsistent in-flight/not-in-flight view.
confirmed.impact: PAR-111 re-entrancy protection (the stated reason for the engine single-flight guard added in this same commit) is silently defeated whenever a benign busy-bounce occurs during a live auto pass. Two overlapping clustering passes can be re-armed, racing the same persons/face_prints writes the guard was added to serialize -- the exact corruption d7b0159f set out to prevent.
confirmed.fix: Stop matching the benign busy kind in the gate-release condition. Either match the specific failure kind(s) instead of a loose substring, e.g. `if (ck.Equals("face_clustering_failed", StringComparison.Ordinal))`, or explicitly exclude the busy notice: `if (ck.Contains("cluster", ...) && !IsNonFatalWarningKind(ck))`. The cleanest is to gate the release on the non-warning branch only -- release the auto gate inside the `else { LastError = ... }` block, never on a LastWarning.

verdict.reasoning:
The introduced bug is real and reachable, but the finding's severity/impact is overstated. Verified facts:

1. Engine emit (main.rs:446-454, d7b0159f): face_clustering_busy is sent as EventPayload::Error, so it lands in the app's `case ErrorEvent e` with e.Error.Kind=="face_clustering_busy". The string contains the substring "cluster".

2. Gate-release (EngineClient.cs:1077-1080): `if (e.Error.Kind is { } ck && ck.Contains("cluster", OrdinalIgnoreCase)) Interlocked.Exchange(ref _faceClusterAutoInFlight, 0);` runs unconditionally after the warning/error split — so even the LastWarning branch (line 1067) hits the gate-clear. `"face_clustering_busy".Contains("cluster")` is true.

3. History: `git log -S` confirms the Contains("cluster") gate-release was added in 550cd194 (predates this commit). Before d7b0159f the only engine kinds matching *cluster* were `face_clustering_failed` (face_clustering.rs:388,403) — a genuine failure where releasing the auto gate is CORRECT. d7b0159f introduced the first BENIGN *cluster* kind, so the loose substring test now mis-fires on it. This is squarely a regression introduced by this commit.

4. Reachability: the manual Re-cluster path (PeopleView.xaml.cs:324 -> RunFaceClusteringAsync -> EngineClient.Commands.cs:299, a bare SendCommandAsync that does NOT touch _faceClusterAutoInFlight) can run while an auto pass started by AutoTriggerFaceClusteringAsync (gate set via CompareExchange(1,0), EngineClient.cs:1224) is live. The engine compare_exchange fails (main.rs:787) and emits busy, and the app then wrongly clears the auto gate mid-flight.

WHERE THE FINDING OVERREACHES (impact): it claims this "defeats PAR-111 ... two overlapping clustering passes ... racing the same persons/face_prints writes — the exact corruption d7b0159f set out to prevent." That worst case is NOT reachable, because the engine's OWN single-flight guard face_cluster_active (added in the SAME commit, main.rs:787-805, FaceClusterActiveGuard dropped only when the spawned task finishes) is the authoritative serialization point and is unaffected by the app-side bug. Any redundant runFaceClustering IPC the corrupted app gate now permits while a real pass runs simply bounces off the engine guard (busy) — it cannot create two concurrent persist transactions. The engine guard releases exactly when the pass completes, at which instant FaceClusteringComplete (face_clustering.rs:380) also clears the app gate (EngineClient.cs:1088), so there is no overlap window. Also, clearing the app gate EARLY can only PERMIT extra passes, never SUPPRESS a needed one, so there is no functional loss (no missed clustering).

Net real effect: a stale/inconsistent app-side state flag that lets through redundant, engine-bounced clustering IPCs plus an extra benign busy warning toast. A genuine introduced correctness regression, but low severity — not the DB-corruption / high-severity outcome claimed.
verdict.fix_assessment: The proposed fix is correct and the right shape. The loose substring match must stop catching the benign busy kind. Best option: release the gate only on the genuine-failure (non-warning) path. Either exclude warnings explicitly with `&& !IsNonFatalWarningKind(ck)`, match the specific failure kind `ck.Equals("face_clustering_failed", StringComparison.Ordinal)`, or move the Interlocked.Exchange into the `else { LastError = ... }` block so it never fires on a LastWarning. All three are valid; the else-branch placement is cleanest because it ties gate-release to "this was a real error" rather than to a fragile substring.

confirmed.title: WaitForMergeSuggestionsAsync resets LastMergeSuggestions to null, flashing "No likely merges found." over the "Looking…" placeholder on every sheet open
confirmed.description: The new WaitForMergeSuggestionsAsync does `LastMergeSuggestions = null;` (line 680) before sending the command, mirroring WaitForBulkActionResultAsync. But unlike LastBulkAction (which no open sheet renders as an empty-state header), LastMergeSuggestions has a persistent, always-subscribed observer: SuggestedMergesSheet.OnEngineChanged (subscribed in the ctor, SuggestedMergesSheet.xaml.cs:36) calls Render() on every LastMergeSuggestions change. The reset to null synchronously fires PropertyChanged -> Render() sees `sug is null` -> sets HeaderText to "No likely merges found. (Try after a fresh scan + re-cluster.)" and clears _rows. This runs immediately AFTER the Loaded handler set "Looking for similar clusters…" (xaml.cs:46), so the user briefly sees the negative "No likely merges found." empty-state while clustering is still running, until the real reply lands and flips it to the count. The pre-d7b0159f code (await FindMergeSuggestionsAsync() with no reset) did not null the property, so this flash is newly introduced. Note the justifying comment ("Reset first so a value-equal reply still re-fires PropertyChanged") does not apply here: MergeSuggestions is a record whose Pairs member is an IReadOnlyList compared by reference, and every IPC reply deserializes a fresh list, so a real reply is never value-equal to the previous and the reset buys nothing while costing the flash.
confirmed.impact: Cosmetic only: a momentary wrong/negative header ("No likely merges found.") between opening the sheet and the suggestions arriving, on every open, including the common case where suggestions do exist. No hang, no NRE.
confirmed.fix: Drop the `LastMergeSuggestions = null;` reset (the reference-typed Pairs means the Set short-circuit can never suppress a genuine reply, so the reset is unnecessary). If a reset is still wanted for the timeout-replay semantics, do it without driving the persistent sheet observer to the empty state -- e.g. have Render() ignore a null transition while a fetch is in flight, or set the placeholder header inside Render() for the null case instead of the negative "No likely merges found." text.

verdict.reasoning:
Verified every claim against git history and current code.

BEFORE (d7b0159f^, SuggestedMergesSheet.xaml.cs Loaded handler): set HeaderText "Looking for similar clusters…", then `await FindMergeSuggestionsAsync()` — a plain fire-and-forget IPC send, NO reset of LastMergeSuggestions. Header stayed on "Looking…" until the real mergeSuggestions reply landed and Render() flipped it to the count.

AFTER (d7b0159f): Loaded handler still sets "Looking…", then `await WaitForMergeSuggestionsAsync(30s)`, which at EngineClient.Commands.cs:680 does `LastMergeSuggestions = null;` before sending the command.

Confirmed mechanism:
- SuggestedMergesSheet subscribes EngineClient.PropertyChanged in its ctor (xaml.cs:36), always live. OnEngineChanged (xaml.cs:72-78) responds to LastMergeSuggestions by DispatcherQueue.TryEnqueue(Render).
- LastMergeSuggestions setter routes through Set<T> (EngineClient.cs:294 -> 1245). Set fires PropertyChanged on any non-equal change; null after a prior non-null value is non-equal, so the null reset DOES fire PropertyChanged.
- Render() (xaml.cs:80-89): `if (sug is null || sug.Pairs.Count == 0)` -> HeaderText = "No likely merges found. (Try after a fresh scan + re-cluster.)" and clears _rows.
- So on every open: "Looking…" -> (enqueued Render from the null reset) "No likely merges found." -> (enqueued Render from real reply) the candidate count. The intermediate negative header is wrong, newly introduced, and shown even in the common case where suggestions DO exist. Because the wait exists precisely because engine clustering can take seconds, the UI thread has ample time to paint and hold "No likely merges found." between the two Renders.

Confirmed the comment's justification is void: MergeSuggestions (Dtos.cs:66) is `record MergeSuggestions(IReadOnlyList<MergeSuggestion> Pairs)`. Record Equals recurses into Pairs via EqualityComparer<IReadOnlyList<MergeSuggestion>>.Default, which for an interface type is reference equality — no structural list comparison. Every reply at EngineClient.cs:1150 deserializes a fresh Suggestions with a fresh Pairs list, so a real reply is never reference-equal to the prior value; the Set short-circuit can never suppress a genuine reply. The reset buys nothing and only costs the flash.

Confirmed sole caller: WaitForMergeSuggestionsAsync is called only from the sheet's Loaded handler (rg), always after the "Looking…" header assignment, so the flash is reachable on every open.

Tried to refute: (a) Could the null reset's PropertyChanged be suppressed? No — null != prior non-null under Set. (b) Could Render from the reset never paint? No — it's a separate dispatcher work item and the real reply is an async IPC round-trip with real latency, leaving time to paint the negative header. (c) Does any other code path need the reset for correctness? No other writer/caller exists. The regression holds. It is genuinely cosmetic/self-correcting (no hang, no NRE, no functional breakage), so severity is honestly low.
verdict.fix_assessment: The finding's primary fix (drop `LastMergeSuggestions = null;` at EngineClient.Commands.cs:680) is correct and sufficient: the reference-typed Pairs guarantees Set never short-circuits a real reply, so the reset has no functional purpose and removing it eliminates the flash entirely. One caveat: the handler at line 673 keys completion on `LastMergeSuggestions is { } r` (non-null). Without the reset, if a *prior* sheet session already left LastMergeSuggestions non-null, the new handler is added AFTER the prior value is already set, so it only fires on the NEXT change (the fresh reply) — which is the desired behavior, so dropping the reset is safe and does not cause a stale/instant-complete. The corrected_fix below is the same drop, plus an optional Render() guard so a future null transition can't repaint the negative empty-state while a fetch is in flight.
